questions.question: Explain the five main segments of C program memory layout with their purposes.
keyPoints: Text/Code Segment - stores compiled machine code instructions
Data Segment - stores initialized global and static variables
BSS Segment - stores uninitialized global and static variables (zero-initialized)
Heap Segment - used for dynamic memory allocation at runtime
Stack Segment - stores function calls, local variables, return addresses

questions.question: What is the difference between Stack and Heap memory? Give at least three differences.
keyPoints: Stack is static allocation (determined at compile time), Heap is dynamic allocation (at runtime)
Stack grows downward, Heap grows upward
Stack is managed automatically, Heap is managed by programmer
Stack has limited size, Heap is limited by system memory
Stack follows LIFO order, Heap does not have ordering
Local variables go to Stack, dynamically allocated variables go to Heap

questions.question: Explain what happens when a function is called in terms of Stack memory allocation.
keyPoints: A new stack frame (activation record) is created
Return address is pushed onto stack
Function parameters are copied onto stack
Space for local variables is allocated
After function returns, stack frame is popped/deallocated

questions.question: Where are the following stored: (a) Global variable, (b) Static variable, (c) Local variable, (d) Dynamic memory pointer variable?
keyPoints: Global variable: Data segment (if initialized) or BSS (if uninitialized)
Static variable: Data segment (if initialized) or BSS (if uninitialized)
Local variable: Stack segment (automatic storage)
Dynamic memory pointer: Stack segment (the pointer itself), actual data in Heap

questions.question: What is BSS segment? Why is it used in memory layout?
keyPoints: BSS stands for Block Started by Symbol
Stores uninitialized global and static variables
OS automatically initializes BSS to zero
More memory-efficient than storing zeros in executable file
Reduces executable file size since only the size of uninitialized data is stored, not actual zeros

questions.question: Explain the concept of stack overflow and heap overflow.
keyPoints: Stack overflow: Stack grows beyond its allocated memory region
Usually caused by excessive recursion or large local arrays
Heap overflow: Heap exhausts available system memory
Caused by excessive dynamic allocation without freeing
Both result in program crash or undefined behavior

questions.question: What are the contents of a stack frame (activation record)?
keyPoints: Return address - where to resume after function returns
Function parameters - values passed to the function
Saved registers - register values to restore later
Local variables - automatic variables declared in function
Previous frame pointer - for stack unwinding during debugging

questions.question: With a neat diagram, explain the complete memory layout of a C program showing all segments and their relative positions in memory addresses.
keyPoints: Diagram showing Text at lowest addresses (0x00000000)
Data segment above Text (initialized globals)
BSS segment above Data (uninitialized globals)
Heap above BSS, grows upward toward Stack
Stack at highest addresses, grows downward toward Heap
Gap between Heap and Stack is unused virtual address space
Mention environmental variables and command-line arguments in Stack
Mention kernel space vs user space distinction
Explain that actual addresses vary with ASLR

questions.question: Write a C program to demonstrate different memory segments. Print addresses of various types of variables to show their storage locations.
keyPoints: Include global variable (Data/BSS)
Include static variable (Data/BSS)
Include local variable (Stack)
Include dynamically allocated memory (Heap)
Use printf with %p to print addresses
Explain how addresses show relative positions in memory
Show that Stack addresses are high, Heap addresses are lower than Stack
Show that global/static addresses are much lower than Stack

questions.question: Explain the memory allocation process when a C program executes, from loading to program termination.
keyPoints: OS creates process and allocates virtual address space
Kernel loads program and creates memory layout
Text segment loaded with machine code
Data segment initialized with values from executable
BSS segment allocated and zero-initialized by OS
Stack created with environment and command-line arguments
Program counter starts at main() in Text segment
Function calls create/destroy stack frames
malloc() allocates from Heap, free() returns to Heap
Program termination releases all resources back to OS

questions.question: Compare static memory allocation and dynamic memory allocation in C with respect to memory segment used, allocation time, advantages, and disadvantages.
keyPoints: Static: compile-time, Dynamic: runtime
Static uses Data/Stack, Dynamic uses Heap
Static: fixed size, Dynamic: size can change
Static: automatic management (no free needed), Dynamic: manual management (must free)
Static advantages: faster, no memory leaks, simpler
Static disadvantages: fixed size, may waste memory
Dynamic advantages: flexible, can handle unknown sizes at compile time
Dynamic disadvantages: slower, memory leaks possible, fragmentation
